description: Placement assessment rubric for evaluating writing ability across narrative, descriptive, and persuasive prompts

levels.4: Writing has a clear beginning, development, and sense of ending; ideas are original and engaging; events connect logically
levels.3: Writing has recognizable structure with a beginning and some development; ideas are clear; most events connect
levels.2: Some attempt at structure but events are loosely connected or incomplete; ideas are present but underdeveloped
levels.1: Little to no structure; writing is a list of disconnected statements or events; ideas are unclear

7-9.4: Has a clear beginning, something happens, and an ending; shows imagination
7-9.3: Has a beginning and something happens; shows some imagination
7-9.2: Starts a story but it trails off or jumps around
7-9.1: Writes sentences that don't form a connected story

10-12.4: Clear narrative arc with setup, rising action, and resolution; engaging premise
10-12.3: Recognizable story structure with beginning, middle, and end
10-12.2: Attempts story structure but missing key parts (e.g., no ending, abrupt shifts)
10-12.1: No recognizable narrative structure; reads as disconnected thoughts

13-15.4: Sophisticated narrative structure; may use non-linear elements, subtext, or thematic coherence; compelling premise
13-15.3: Solid narrative arc with clear setup, development, and resolution
13-15.2: Basic structure present but predictable or underdeveloped
13-15.1: Minimal narrative structure; lacks intentional organization

levels.4: Distinct voice comes through; characters feel real through action, dialogue, or thought; writing has personality
levels.3: Some voice present; characters are named or described; writing shows some personality
levels.2: Flat or generic voice; characters exist but feel like placeholders; writing lacks personality
levels.1: No discernible voice; no real characters; writing feels mechanical or copied

7-9.4: Writing sounds like THEM -- playful, curious, or excited; characters do things and have feelings
7-9.3: Writing has some personality; characters are mentioned with at least one detail
7-9.2: Writing is flat but functional; characters are named but not developed
7-9.1: Writing feels robotic or copied; no characters distinguishable

10-12.4: Strong individual voice; characters have motivations and reactions; dialogue or internal thought present
10-12.3: Emerging voice; characters have basic traits; some personality in writing style
10-12.2: Generic voice; characters are generic ('a boy', 'she'); no real personality
10-12.1: No voice; characters absent or purely functional

13-15.4: Distinctive authorial voice; nuanced characters with inner life; tone is intentional and controlled
13-15.3: Clear voice and style; characters have some depth; tone is mostly consistent
13-15.2: Voice is emerging but inconsistent; characters are one-dimensional
13-15.1: No discernible voice or style; writing is purely functional

levels.4: Varied sentence structures; age-appropriate vocabulary used effectively; minimal errors that don't impede meaning
levels.3: Some sentence variety; vocabulary is adequate; errors present but meaning is clear
levels.2: Simple or repetitive sentences; limited vocabulary; errors sometimes impede meaning
levels.1: Fragmented or run-on sentences throughout; very limited vocabulary; frequent errors impede meaning

7-9.4: Writes in complete sentences; uses some interesting words; spelling/punctuation mostly correct for age
7-9.3: Mostly complete sentences; basic vocabulary; some spelling/punctuation errors typical for age
7-9.2: Mix of complete and incomplete sentences; very basic vocabulary; many errors but still readable
7-9.1: Mostly fragments or extremely simple sentences; minimal vocabulary; hard to read

10-12.4: Varied sentence lengths and types; uses descriptive or precise vocabulary; minor mechanical errors
10-12.3: Some sentence variety; adequate vocabulary; mechanical errors don't impede understanding
10-12.2: Repetitive sentence patterns; limited vocabulary; noticeable errors affect flow
10-12.1: Little sentence control; very basic vocabulary; errors significantly impede reading

13-15.4: Sophisticated sentence structures; precise vocabulary with intentional word choice; minimal errors
13-15.3: Good sentence variety; appropriate vocabulary; occasional errors
13-15.2: Basic sentence structures; adequate but unambitious vocabulary; frequent errors
13-15.1: Poor sentence control; limited vocabulary; pervasive errors

levels.4: Uses multiple senses (sight, sound, touch, smell, taste) with specific, vivid details that create a strong mental picture
levels.3: Uses at least two senses with some specific details; reader can picture the subject
levels.2: Relies on one sense (usually sight) or uses generic details; picture is vague
levels.1: Tells rather than describes; no sensory language; just labels things

7-9.4: Mentions what they see, hear, AND feel/smell/taste; uses specific words ('crunchy red apple' not just 'apple')
7-9.3: Uses two senses; has some specific words
7-9.2: Mostly tells what things look like; uses common words ('big', 'nice', 'good')
7-9.1: Lists things without describing them ('There was a tree. There was a house.')

10-12.4: Rich sensory layering across 3+ senses; details are precise and evocative
10-12.3: Uses 2-3 senses; details are specific enough to form a mental image
10-12.2: Some sensory language but generic ('it smelled good', 'it was loud')
10-12.1: No real sensory engagement; writing tells rather than shows

13-15.4: Masterful sensory selection -- chooses details for emotional and thematic effect, not just coverage; unexpected sense pairings
13-15.3: Effective sensory writing across multiple senses; details serve a descriptive purpose
13-15.2: Sensory language is present but predictable or surface-level
13-15.1: Little to no sensory engagement; abstract rather than concrete

levels.4: Words are chosen for precision and effect; avoids vague or overused words; language feels intentional
levels.3: Some precise word choices; mostly avoids the vaguest terms; shows effort to describe well
levels.2: Relies on common/vague words (nice, good, big, stuff); little precision
levels.1: Very limited vocabulary; words are functional, not descriptive

7-9.4: Uses interesting describing words beyond 'big/small/nice'; tries new words
7-9.3: Uses some describing words; occasionally specific
7-9.1: Barely any adjectives or describing words

10-12.4: Chooses specific verbs and adjectives; avoids cliches; words create mood
10-12.3: Some strong word choices mixed with generic ones
10-12.2: Mostly generic vocabulary; occasional interesting word

13-15.4: Sophisticated, intentional vocabulary; connotation and denotation both considered; language creates texture
13-15.3: Good vocabulary range; words mostly serve their purpose well
13-15.2: Adequate vocabulary but uninspired; no risk-taking in word choice
13-15.1: Limited or imprecise vocabulary; words don't serve the description

levels.4: Creates vivid mental images through concrete details, comparisons, or figurative language that enhances understanding
levels.3: Some imagery present; may include a comparison or figurative language attempt
levels.2: Limited imagery; writing is more telling than showing; no figurative language
levels.1: No imagery; purely functional or abstract language

7-9.4: Uses at least one comparison ('like a...' or 'as...as') that helps the reader picture something; creates a clear mental picture
7-9.3: Tries to paint a picture with words; may have one comparison
7-9.2: Doesn't try to create mental images; just states facts
7-9.1: No attempt at imagery or comparison

10-12.4: Uses simile, metaphor, or personification effectively; imagery creates atmosphere
10-12.3: Includes figurative language or strong imagery; some of it is effective
10-12.2: Minimal imagery; figurative language absent or cliched
10-12.1: No figurative language or imagery; writing is entirely literal and flat

13-15.4: Original figurative language that illuminates subject; extended imagery or symbolism; shows craft awareness
13-15.3: Effective use of figurative language; imagery serves a clear purpose
13-15.2: Some figurative language but predictable or decorative rather than functional

levels.4: Position is crystal clear from the start; argument is focused and doesn't wander; reader knows exactly what is being argued and why
levels.3: Position is stated and mostly maintained; argument has some focus
levels.2: Position is vague or shifts; argument wanders between points
levels.1: No clear position; hard to tell what is being argued

7-9.4: Clearly says what they want or believe AND gives a reason; stays focused on one idea
7-9.3: States an opinion; gives at least one reason
7-9.2: Has an opinion but it's hard to find or changes; reasons are weak
7-9.1: No clear opinion; just describes the topic without taking a side

10-12.4: Clear thesis/claim; argument is structured with distinct reasons; maintains focus throughout
10-12.3: Position is clear; has at least two supporting reasons
10-12.2: Position exists but is vague; reasons are present but unfocused

13-15.4: Sophisticated, nuanced thesis; argument is logically structured with clear progression; shows awareness of complexity
13-15.3: Clear position with logical support; argument flows well
13-15.2: Position is stated but argument lacks depth or logical structure
13-15.1: No clear argumentative stance; writing is informational rather than persuasive

levels.4: Supports claims with specific evidence (examples, facts, personal experience); explains WHY the evidence matters
levels.3: Provides some evidence or examples; connection to claim is present
levels.2: Claims are made but evidence is weak, vague, or missing; 'because I think so' reasoning
levels.1: No evidence; pure assertion without support

7-9.4: Gives at least two specific reasons; reasons make sense and connect to their opinion
7-9.3: Gives at least one specific reason that connects to their opinion
7-9.2: Gives a reason but it's very vague ('because it's fun') or doesn't connect
7-9.1: No reasons given; just states opinion

10-12.4: Multiple pieces of evidence (examples, facts, experiences); reasoning explains the connection between evidence and claim
10-12.3: At least two pieces of evidence; some reasoning present
10-12.2: Evidence is thin or generic; reasoning is implicit
10-12.1: No supporting evidence; argument is unsupported assertion

13-15.4: Strong, varied evidence with explicit reasoning; may anticipate counterarguments; shows logical sophistication
13-15.3: Adequate evidence with clear reasoning; argument is logically sound
13-15.2: Some evidence but reasoning is weak or assumptions unstated
13-15.1: Assertions without evidence or logical support

levels.4: Writing is genuinely persuasive -- reader feels pulled toward the position; uses rhetorical techniques appropriate for age
levels.3: Writing has some persuasive energy; reads as more than just a report of opinions
levels.2: Writing states opinions but doesn't actively try to persuade; reads like a list of facts
levels.1: Not persuasive; reads as neutral description or disconnected statements

7-9.4: Sounds like they really care about their opinion; enthusiasm and conviction come through; may address the reader directly
7-9.3: Shows some feeling about the topic; opinion is expressed with some energy
7-9.2: States opinion flatly without much feeling; doesn't try to convince

10-12.4: Uses persuasive techniques (rhetorical questions, emotional appeals, direct address); voice is confident and compelling
10-12.3: Some persuasive techniques; voice shows conviction
10-12.2: Mostly informational tone; little active persuasion
10-12.1: No persuasive voice; reads as a neutral report

13-15.4: Sophisticated rhetorical awareness; voice balances logic and emotion; may use irony, analogy, or strategic concession; genuinely compelling
13-15.3: Effective persuasive voice; uses some rhetorical devices; comes across as convincing
13-15.2: Some persuasive intent but execution is weak; relies on assertion over rhetoric
13-15.1: No rhetorical awareness; flat, unconvincing tone

effort_engagement.description: A holistic modifier applied after per-dimension scoring to account for genuine effort vs. gaming/disengagement
indicators_of_genuine_effort: Writing shows personal investment (unique ideas, personal voice, specific details)
Length is appropriate for the child's age, even if quality is low
Content is on-topic and responsive to the prompt
Errors appear to be developmental, not carelessness
indicators_of_low_effort_or_gaming: Extremely short responses (well below age-appropriate minimum)
Repeated sentences, filler text, or keyboard mashing
Copied text from the prompt itself
Off-topic content unrelated to the prompt
Responses that feel AI-generated (unnaturally sophisticated vocabulary, formulaic structure unlike a child's writing)
effort_engagement.scoring_impact: Do NOT adjust per-dimension scores for effort. Instead, set confidence lower (0.3-0.5) and note effort concerns in the reasoning field. The tier recommendation should still reflect observed writing ability.
